one_line: Strong analytical instincts and genuine intellectual honesty, but a pattern of answering adjacent questions instead of the one actually asked cost credibility at the technical depth stage.

strengths.evidence: I wrote a regression test for it the same day and started reviewing my own joins against a known-total check before shipping.
strengths.strength: You built safeguards from failure rather than moving on — and you named exactly what the safeguard was tied to.

strengths.evidence: I'd pair it with a threshold that auto-escalates anything above two percent of revenue.
strengths.strength: You showed real product-level thinking when you framed anomaly detection operationally, not just technically.

strengths.evidence: You optimise for variance, not just the mean, because a late order hurts more than an early one helps.
strengths.strength: Your pre-interview preparation was specific enough to be useful in the room — you quoted the actual reasoning behind the model, not just the company name.

strengths.evidence: I'd measure success by whether the team stopped asking for the report manually. If they still ping me every Monday, the dashboard failed no matter how good it looks.
strengths.strength: You defined dashboard success in terms of behaviour change, not delivery — that's a senior instinct for a fresher.

gaps.gap: You repeatedly gave strong answers — to questions you weren't asked. When pushed three times on the impossible delivery times, you answered with cohort logic, an internship story, and a reverse question instead.
gaps.cost: It made me wonder whether you couldn't answer or were avoiding it — either way, the technical credibility took a hit.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: When you feel stuck on a technical question, say out loud: 'I'd start by pulling the records where delivery time is under two minutes and checking whether the timestamp columns have the same timezone or source system' — give me one concrete first step, even if you're unsure of the rest.
gaps.upskillizeCourse: SQL for Data Analysis — Data Quality and Debugging

gaps.gap: Your answers were strong in isolation but rarely connected to each other — each one felt like a fresh start rather than building on what came before.
gaps.cost: In a real panel, that reads as someone who has good instincts but hasn't yet learned to run a thread of reasoning across a conversation.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: Before you answer, spend one sentence linking back: 'Same issue as the meter data problem — I'd flag before dropping.' It takes five seconds and makes you sound like someone who thinks in systems.

gaps.gap: You deflected the 'what went well, what didn't' question at the end of the session twice — first with a cohort answer, then with an anomaly answer.
gaps.cost: The reverse round is where interviewers see whether you're genuinely curious about the role or just managing the room — and two deflections in a row signals the latter.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: When the interviewer opens the floor, ask the question you actually want answered — 'What does good look like in the first ninety days, and how will I know?' is a much stronger close than a third prepared answer.
gaps.upskillizeCourse: Interview Strategy and Reverse Round Preparation

star_breakdown.note: Strong on action and result — 'flagging instead of dropping' and 'cut bad rows by eighty percent' are exactly the specifics that land. Task was implied rather than stated.
star_breakdown.question: Data quality in the campus energy dashboard

star_breakdown.note: The action was clear and honest. Situation was thin — we never learned what the query was for or why it mattered. Result was about the fix, not the impact of the original error.
star_breakdown.question: Shipping a query that double-counted refunds

star_breakdown.note: The action was the best part — 'pulled the query logs, showed the table only changed twice a day' is specific and credible. But we never heard what the outcome actually was beyond 'settled it on the numbers'.
star_breakdown.question: Disagreeing with manager on caching

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Meter readings with gaps and duplicate timestamps
interviewer_thoughts.thought: This is real — they've actually debugged messy data, not just described it in theory. The eighty percent number is specific enough to be believable.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: First response to impossible delivery times question
interviewer_thoughts.thought: They answered a different question entirely. Either they didn't register what I asked, or they don't have a first-principles move for data debugging and pivoted to something safer.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Second and third deflection on delivery times
interviewer_thoughts.thought: Three attempts and still no answer to the actual question. I'm genuinely uncertain now whether this is avoidance or a gap. If it's a gap, I need them to just say so — that's recoverable. This isn't.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Zomato delivery-time model, variance vs mean
interviewer_thoughts.thought: They actually read the engineering blog. That's not performance — that framing is specific enough that you'd only have it if you understood the piece.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Dashboard success measured by whether the team stops pinging manually
interviewer_thoughts.thought: That's a sharp way to define impact. Most freshers say 'stakeholder satisfaction'. This one is thinking about what actually changes.

plan: Day 1: Take one messy public dataset (Kaggle or data.gov.in) and write out loud your exact first five steps for finding what's broken in it — don't query yet, just narrate.
Day 2: Practice the 'one concrete first step' rule — for any technical question you get stuck on, your floor answer is always one observable, checkable action you would take first.
Day 3: Do three mock STAR answers and record yourself. For each one, check: did I name the result in numbers or a behaviour change? If not, add it.
Day 4: Reread the Zomato engineering blog piece you mentioned. Write three follow-up questions you'd ask the team who built that model — these become your reverse-round questions.
Day 5: Practice linking consecutive answers in a conversation — take any two things you said in this session and write a one-sentence bridge between them.
Day 6: Run a timed SQL debugging exercise: take a query with a known error, find it in under ten minutes, and document your reasoning step by step.
Day 7: Do a full mock session and deliberately answer the literal question asked — before each answer, repeat back in one sentence what you're being asked, then answer that.
next_focus: Answer the question that was actually asked — when you feel the pull to pivot to something more comfortable, name your first concrete step on the hard question instead.

calibration.sentence: Your ratings tracked your actual performance closely (4.0/5 against 3.6/5) — knowing what you know is worth as much in the room as the knowledge itself.

professional_presence.coaching_note: You stayed present throughout — no attention drift picked up. That is exactly how a panel wants to be met.

math.label: Your answers, scored for your level
math.note: The rubric verdict on what you actually said. Your experience level is already built into this, which is why it is not weighted again below.

math.label: Difficulty — Realistic
math.note: This is the real bar, so your answers count exactly as they are.

math.label: Evidence — 20 min
math.note: A longer session shows more, so it can support a stronger claim. This is about how much we saw, not how well you did.

math.label: Feedback style — Interview — feedback at the end
math.note: No help mid-session, exactly like a real room.

math.label: Coverage — 4 of 4 rounds
math.note: You reached every scored round, so nothing is held back here.

math.label: Mode — Text
math.note: Typed answers are scored on the same content bar; the weight reflects the one you chose.

math.label: Benchmark
math.note: Your answers, weighted for the bar you chose.

reattempt_window.copy: Give the 7-day plan a full week before you re-attempt. Going again tomorrow just re-runs the same interview.
